data.title: Intel Nicely On Target, More Required
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) reported on the 22nd of January, after which the stock price crashed almost twenty percent. Should investors be worried? Was the report that bad? It has since recovered nicely, but slowly trending downward once again. In our view, Intel is more than on track. Investors, traders, momentum players, simply drove the stock price into unreasonable values, the $50s. Of most importance, the conference call included a statement from the CEO, Lip-Bu Tan,

In addition, PC become an important part of the AI infrastructure. The surge in AI workloads is driving massive demand for data centers, but cloud capacity alone cannot meet the scale of inference needed especially in the power constrained environment. This accelerating the push toward hybrid AI, splitting workloads between cloud and client which offers clear advantages in performance, cost and control. We are working closely with ecosystem partners to seamlessly enable hybrid AI, and we are encouraged by the opportunity to grow the installed base and accelerate the refresh rates over time.

Wise investors might have picked up on the importance. All required strategy for a bright future was included. It is rich with intellectual business wisdom. When Intel's hardware manufacturing capability, fab, gets added, growth power becomes compelling. The synergy pieces now combine in a likely financial explosive growth move forward. We could end with this and just write, go buy. Obviously, that thought sells shortcoming values. Tan and the former CEO, both understood the immense value within the organization, while the skeptical financial analysts wreaked havoc upon the strategy, demanding a breakup. The CEO, also, added one valuable cultural necessity: simplify. He demanded that products match markets with the fewest possible implementations. This, in time, pays investors huge dividends, huge.

Maybe it is time to review the targets and check further on the arrow's path.

A Major Issue

The call outlined one major issue, lack of 86x capacity. Demand exceeds capacity, leaving shareholders with a relatively awkward view of the future. Management decided several quarters ago to forgo investment in any additional capacity for the 86x products. This was driven by a still bloated balance sheet and tight cash flow environment. The company understood that the value and solution resided within the 18A/14A technology, which, when built out, offered relief from capacity dearth as new products moved thusly. This decision to not expand 86x capacity adds pressure for Intel to actually succeed with 18A.

The Acronyms Table

Intel's broad reach creates an acronym nightmare. Attempting to provide clarity, we copied an updated table from a previous article, intending to add meaning.

Process Summary * Intel 3 Intel 4 Intel 18A
Granite Check
Meteor Lake Check Check
Lunar Lake TSMC 3NM
Arrow Lake TSMC 3NM
Panther Lake Check
NOVA Lake Check plus TSMC
Grand Rapids Check Check
Clearwater Forest Check
Jaguar Shores Check

* Fab shop is moving rapidly to EUV processes.

Next, a table contains products by their three major businesses: computing (EDGE), data center, and AI. The table is ordered loosely by time.

Business Products Products By Time 2025 2026
Edge Meteor Lake Arrow Lake Lunar Lake Panther Lake (Core Ultra Series 3) 18A

NOVA Lake

(Year-end) 18A
Data Center/Servers Grand Rapids Clearwater Forest
AI Gaudi Jaguar Shores (Future)

Term definition follows:

  • NEX - Network and Edge Group.
  • CCG - Client Computing Group.
  • DCAI - Data Center and AI Group.
  • IMS - Nanofabrication business.
  • SCIP - Semiconductor Co-Investment Program (Smart capital approach.)
  • Intel Products - incudes CCG, DCAI, and NEX.
  • Core Ultra - Integrated NPU & CPU.
  • Xeon® 6/7 - Server processors family.

A Pathway with Synergy

In our opening, we included a statement from the CEO that summarized these important concepts:

  • Cloud computing can't carry the complete AI load, especially when considering the power.
  • Hybrid approaches become required.
    • Splits workloads between cloud and client.
      • Offers clear advantages in performance, cost, and control.
      • Apple's (AAPL) approach: optimally locates functions at the proper level, an Apple server or mobile device.

When considering edge versus cloud, several factors play roles. Edge can be defined:

Edge AI refers to the process of using AI algorithms and AI models on edge or Internet of Things (IoT) devices like smartphones, thermostats and wearable health monitors. Edge AI gets its name from edge computing, a type of distributed computing that brings applications closer to data sources.

Cloud computing can be defined as:

In cloud AI, data is collected at its source and moved into the cloud through an internet connection. There it can access virtual compute resources connected for data processing, data analysis and data storage.

Huge differences exist between the two. Edge fits best with latency issues, or compiling large amounts of data such as in analog-to-digital conversion, or maintaining strict control of privacy, or offline mode. Cloud interactions fit best when vast amounts of information scattered across multiple websites are required. Both are needed, especially in trying to optimize internet traffic. When considering the holistic system, a separation of functionality, a key function, exists with software placed at one location knowing how to couple with the right software located elsewhere without opening access to unwanted interference (hacking). This has become a battleground for AI. Our past experience suggests that when one entity controls and builds all of the pieces, issues such as this find more robust solutions. Intel is heading into this posture quite quickly. Also, Intel is like Apple building processors that contain both the CPU and NPUs with its hybrid approach, simplifying.

On this subject of inner conductivity, Lip-Bu Tan, CEO, offered this comment at a recent conference,

And then, of course, the [computer] compatibility is very important. And even more important, right now, it's a cluster of GPU or CPU and then it becomes the clustered manager, the software. And right now, sometimes if we have a problem, you don't even know where to look for it. And now there's a lot of new start-up coming to me, they said, Li-Bu, that will be an area that we want to work on so that we know exactly how to solve some of the clustered issue that you're going to face. . . .

Intel's ability to provide a holistic solution of hardware mix is also complemented by its hardware fab capability. In the conference call, management stated:

And build on our most advanced Intel 18A manufacturing process. We committed to deliver our first Series 3 SKU by the end of 2025, and we exceeded that commitment by delivering our first 3 SKUs. While we still have work to do, I'm encouraged by the steady progress on our Intel 18A use, and Naga and his team remain laser-focused on additional improvements as they ramp Series 3 into the high volume needed to meet strong customer demand.

This 18A technology, with its other features, makes it state-of-the-art on a worldwide basis.

The Synergy

Simply put, Intel controls all of the critical pieces with progressing state-of-the-art manufacturing and quickly moving forward with competitive products. An example from 9to5, Latest Intel chips beat Apple’s M5 in multi-core scores – for maybe two days, illustrates the massive progress gained in the past few years. The company still hasn't yet released all of the details for the AI total solution and plans, but many of the pieces are in place.

1st Quarter Guidance

The 4th quarter report is now old news, but the guidance given isn't. A slide with the 1st quarter guidance follows next.

Notice the net zero earnings projection. In part, this arises from the massive 18A startup costs. In the early quarters, 18A low volumes will significantly crush margins until volumes increase into the second half of the year. Management is targeting 40% - 60% margins versus the 34% guided for the 1st quarter going forward. This is a key to follow. Also, following the balance sheet is critical. On this issue, David Zinsner, CFO, added this comment.

As we think about our capital expenditures for 2026, we're working to balance our ability to drive capital efficiencies with our need to respond to the demand signals we're receiving. Previously, we said CapEx would be down but are now planning for a range of flat to down slightly and for expenditures to be more weighted to the first half.

Intel closed the year with $44B in long-term debt, slightly lower than the year before, with $15B in cash. Watching this difference through the next few years is important.

Yields

Perhaps, the most important statement made in recent times came from the CEO at the AI conference. When asked about 18A yield, Tan opened with this:

And we have the 18A. And when I took over, the yield is quite poor. And so I really get all my friends to help from PDF solution to KLA in the equipment to make sure that we have that 7% to 8% yield improvement per month.

He follows with this revelation:

And that will be the best practice. And it took me a while. Finally, now I seeing 7% or 8% yield per month. So in a way, it's very helpful that you open up the kimono and let people come and help you. And then -- so I think one is 18A. We just announced the [Panther] Lake, and then I can really count on my fab to produce that. And then interesting enough, a couple of customers are knocking on my door now and say, hey, it seems like your 18A is doing well. We want to be part of that.

Yields are improving massively. The CEO then discussed 14A and the company's laser focus on it. Without naming the customers, he noted that two are very much interested. One of those customers has been rumored to be Apple. Risk production begins in 28 and volume in 29. Also, again without naming any potential customers, the discussion around having mobile IP presented itself. Intel certainly plans for an inclusion of this capability. In other words, the CEO was hinting strongly about 14A being targeted at mobile customers. Continuing, "So that's something that is missing in the past. So, I really put something in place so that we can have both and then serve the customer. The good news is a couple of customers are very excited."

Pathway Forward

Tan, also, offered a brief look at the longer-term future, stating,

[I]t gives me that kind of perspective of what are the new things coming and quantum computing is another big area coming up is around the corner. And so something that is interesting to see after AI, we talk about agentic AI, the next big wave is the physical. The next thing is going to be quantum. So I think that's kind of exciting.

We will let readers go research this topic further as it is rather deep and lengthy. In a very short description, this technology takes advantage of nature's quantum mechanics laws. Clearly, Tan had in mind products that will eventually support even this technology.

Risk

There is always risk. For Intel, two in particular come to mind: margins and debt. Following those two issues is of prime importance. But the 4th quarter delivered major steps forward with both cash flows and improving the balance sheet. Another risk involves more demand than capacity. It is happening and will continue until enough products are moved to 18A sometime in 2027. Also, Tan discussed at AI the difficulties in running two different companies, fab vs. products. He noted that the fab culture is grinding and grinding and grinding while the product is about innovation. It is a struggle to keep both sides focused on the right path. It is also a risk that seems to be now in check.

Summary

In summary, Intel has a very bright future. The business is in perfect synergy. The question becomes entry prices. Is the current price near $45 expensive? In our view, it is. Analyst's estimate for this year predicts $0.45 and next year $1. The current P/E at this year's earnings is close to 100. The general market is much overvalued in the stochastics. The Intel chart included shows a bearish pattern likely forming.

A careful inspection suggests that if the price breaks down under $44 - $43, the pattern suggests that $36 - $38 is quite possible. We rate Intel a hold until the charts start to show underbought stochastic in at least the week bar but would prefer to see one in the month bar also.

We believe that patience is truly in order for now. But the long-term story continues to unfold, and in our view, Intel will shine again in time.

data.title: Buy Shipping: Undervalued Overlooked Beneficiary Of Geopolitical Tensions
data.description:
The past few years have seen geopolitical tensions become far more acute than in the early 2010s, when it seemed a lingering financial recession was the biggest worry. This new environment has also led to many investment plays, as an example of one that I covered early on before it became a multibaggers: MP Materials (MP), which I initiated a buy recommendation in January 2024 (since up by 226%)

In this article I’m going to discuss another sector that I believe will massively benefit from geopolitical tensions but is currently massively undervalued: shipping. I believe shipping rates will potentially undergo a huge secular bull market, and companies that own ships will benefit. My bullish thesis rests on several pillars discussed below.

1. Low shipping rates are an anomaly

Shipping rates have been very low in the past four decades when compared to historical levels, as shown below (source). Despite four decades of inflation and changes in the shipping market, the BDI is not much different than it was four decades ago. Now, a direct comparison between the index in 2025 and 1985 is not possible due to technical reasons such as index calculation changes, but at least it should give a directional sense.

Shipping is still a fairly concentrated business, top 3 companies account for 46% of global market share, and the top 5 companies account for about 64%. Typically such levels of market concentration would give major industry players pricing power, yet prices have been more or less stagnant over decades and underperform even official inflation ($1 in 1985 is worth $3 in 2026 per official statistics).

I believe the key stagnant shipping rate environment is primarily driven by China vastly increasing the supply of ships as part of its overall economic strategy to grow foreign trade and its economy. In this sense, shipping is a “public good” or “basic infrastructure” provided at a subsidized level (to minimize “transaction costs”) with a bigger aim (overall economic growth) in mind. In the early 2000s, China-owned merchant ships accounted for approximately 5% of the world fleet. By late 2024, this share grew to nearly 20% of global gross tonnage. In 2024, China accounted for 53.3% of global shipbuilding output. By 2025, it secured roughly 69% of all new global ship orders.

China’s state-owned shipping company (China Overseas Shipping Company, “COSCO,” which has a global market share of 10.8% in 2025) barely eked out any profit from 2011 to 2019 before the COVID boom starting in 2020 (in fact, if you add all the losses and profits during 2011 to 2019, you end up with a net loss). It has not been operated to generate a profit despite an asset size of nearly $70 billion.

But I believe this anomalous period of low shipping rates may be coming to an end, as it no longer makes sense for China to provide “limitless supply.” This brings me to my next point.

2. Geopolitical tensions change the calculus for China

Recent events are changing the calculus for China and may cause it to no longer prioritize providing shipping at a low cost:

  • Panama cancelled the contract under which Hong Kong company CK Hutchison (owned by famous tycoon Li Ka-shing) operated two ports. Incidentally, the ports are contemplated to transfer to Maersk, so it appears a contender was already waiting in the wings.
  • Australia is also considering reclaiming China’s leasehold of the port of Darwin.
  • Chinese ownership of the Greek port in Piraeus is also under pressure, as shown here (source).

These examples show that the geopolitical competition over strategic ports and, with it, access to waterways and oceanic trade is growing white-hot. Faced with these uncertainties, it may make less sense for China to expand its merchant fleet with its past vigor. Incidentally, China has also reduced its other infrastructure spending and capped loss-making infrastructure spending as fiscal deficits are persistently high and economic growth slows. Under such an environment, I believe it will become increasingly likely that China will expand its merchant fleet more cautiously, if at all.

Peter Zeihan (whose books are contentious but have been spot-on in many occasions about the changes facing the current world) suggests that as a result of the changing world order, countries will eventually prey on merchant shipping of unfriendly nations. While we don’t see that yet, it could also be a catalyst in the future.

3. Near-term catalysts

Near-term catalysts also include current geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. The Houthis (widely assumed to be Iranian proxies) have not been disrupting Red Sea ships for a while since the ceasefire, and the US and Iran are currently undergoing negotiations. If these go poorly, as some news reports have suggested, in the very least the Houthis might go back to disrupting shipping in the Red Sea, shutting down that route for the foreseeable future and delaying the resumption of shipping in the Red Sea.

4. Asymmetrical upside

I see asymmetrical upside in shipping stocks, for example, Maersk (AMKBY), which is the world’s second-largest shipping company with a global market share of 14.6% in 2025. AMKBY has a market cap of $35 billion and has been relatively flat over the last 20 or so years. It trades at a P/E of 13 though near term profitability means relatively little given the cyclicality of the industry. Note that it earned $29 bn in 2022 ($18 bn in 2021) in just two years during the pandemic before falling to $3bn in 2025 as shipping rates reverted to lower levels. However, if shipping rates increase to 2022 levels on a sustained level and Maersk earns $29 bn/year priced at 10 of P/E, the market cap could go to $300 bn. Maersk is the biggest shipping company that’s listed, so I’m using it as an example; other freight companies may also benefit asymmetrically. If this trade doesn’t work out, Maersk likely won’t lose a lot of value either if it trades at the prices it has traded at in the past two decades.

Risks to bullish thesis

The above scenarios are somewhat speculative, so it could very well be that China continues to expand shipping in search of foreign markets (even if faced with challenges such as port concessions getting reclaimed by the host countries). Shipping rates and shipping stocks could limp along at current prices or lower for indefinite periods in such a scenario.

Conclusion

I’m currently long some form of long-dated shipping futures in my own portfolio. While some readers may feel I am talking my own book, I am also putting my money where my mouth is (same as for MP). Now I don’t necessarily think this will play out, but the risk-reward is favorable (major potential upside in my view, compared to the downside risk). However, for individual investors, I would suggest considering large-cap shipping companies such as Maersk.

data.title: Western Union Remains An Income And Value Trap Following Its 2025 Earnings
data.description:
As I covered a couple of months ago, while Western Union (WU) offers a very high dividend yield, this seems to be a trap, as the company’s fundamental issues aren’t easy to fix and there isn’t much value for long-term investors in my opinion. Not surprisingly, its shares have performed somewhat poorly since then, being down by 1.5% when including dividends and underperforming the overall stock market (SPY) during the same period.

This trend has not changed much today when the company released its financial figures related to 2025, with its share price reacting negatively to a revenue miss in the last quarter of 2025, even though, on the other hand, its guidance for 2026 was broadly in line with market expectations.

During 2025, Western Union’s total revenues amounted to $4.1 billion, representing a decline of 4% YoY. When adjusted for the negative impact of the suspension of its services in Iraq back in 2023, revenue declined by 2% compared to 2024, which shows that Iraq had some negative effect on revenue growth. However, as shown in the next graph, in the last quarter of 2025, its revenues declined by 5% YoY and Iraq didn’t have a meaningful impact, being a worrisome trend for its revenues ahead.

This is justified by declining revenues in its core business of money transfers worldwide, especially in North America, where the company is seeing more weakness than compared to other geographies. This is largely explained by U.S. immigration policies in recent months that have tightened controls against immigrants, which have a natural negative impact on cash remittances. Indeed, the company said during its earnings conference call that it saw particular weakness in remittances from the U.S. to Mexico, with migrants likely being less willing to visit physical agent locations. Given that this is Western Union’s core business, stricter U.S. immigration policies have a direct impact on its business, something that only adds more cyclical pressure on a business that is already in structural decline. Having said that, according to the company, on a monthly basis November was particularly weak for transactions to Mexico (-6% YoY), but a rebound happened in December (+2% YoY), even though monthly transactions are volatile and the overall trend has remained negative over the last year.

Acknowledging the structural headwinds in its core retail remittances business, the company’s strategy to offset these issues has been focused on digitalization over the past few years. It has developed new digital products and services to better compete against new competitors, namely fintech companies that usually offer a cheaper and more efficient way to transfer money than through traditional channels.

This strategy has been important for the company to grow other revenue streams, namely those related to branded digital transactions and consumer services.

Branded digital transactions were up by 6% in Q4 2025, and consumer services revenues increased by 15%, clearly being the company’s growth engines. For the full year, the consumer services unit reported revenues of $543 million, up by an impressive 32% YoY, but this was not enough to offset declining revenues in the consumer money transfer segment (which includes branded digital transactions) that reported revenues of $3.5 billion (-8% YoY).

Despite its efforts to push for growth in digital transactions and services, Western Union’s revenues are still 86% generated from the consumer money transfer unit, which is likely to remain a drag on revenue growth over the medium to long term. Additionally, Western Union reached an agreement a few months ago to buy Intermex (IMXI) for about $500 million, a deal that will increase its presence in the cash retail remittances segment. In my opinion, this is not the best move from a strategic point of view, as it would make much more sense to grow in digital activities, even though from a geographical perspective it will increase its presence in Central and Latin America and it will reduce its still large exposure to North America.

Another area that has been important to offset revenue weakness is related to operational efficiencies, as the company delivered significant expense reductions during last year. Indeed, Western Union has pursued some cost-cutting initiatives related to retail footprint optimization, overhead reductions, and process automations, resulting in a decline of 14% YoY in selling, general, and administrative expenses to $742 million during 2025. This was quite important for the company to report a slightly higher operating margin despite top-line pressures during last year, which can be considered a good outcome given the challenging operating environment over the past few quarters.

This is a trend that is likely to be maintained ahead, as the company is expected to complete the Intermex acquisition in the coming months. This deal is expected to deliver some $30 million in annual cost synergies within two years, being an important support for its business margins ahead.

Given this combination of lower revenues, but also declining operating expenses, its operating income increased by 4% YoY to $757 million in 2025. Regarding its net income, Western Union reported a drop of 47% YoY to $499 million in 2025, but this is explained by a large one-time tax benefit related to the reorganization of its international operations in 2024. Adjusted for this effect, its adjusted net income was $574 million during last year, representing a decline of 3.4% YoY.

Regarding its cash flow generation, cash from operations amounted to $544 million in 2025 (vs. $406 million in 2024), with the difference being largely related to taxes. Its capex was about $151 million, up by 15% from the previous year, as the company invested more in technology infrastructure and also had higher agent wins and renewals.

Therefore, its free cash flow amounted to about $393 million, which was enough to finance its dividend distributions of $300 million during the year but not enough to also fund the share buyback program of $225 million. This means Western Union is increasing its financial leverage to return capital to shareholders, which doesn’t seem to be the best capital allocation in my opinion, as the company faces significant issues ahead and should be investing more in growing its business rather than raising debt to return capital to shareholders.

Moreover, its financial leverage is expected to increase when the Intermex acquisition completes over the coming months, to a ratio around 2.1x, which seems to be somewhat high for a company with a structurally declining business. Not surprisingly, Western Union only expects to make share repurchases of about $100 million during 2026, as the balance sheet becomes more leveraged and interest costs are expected to increase as the company refinances old debt that had much lower costs compared to the current interest rate environment.

While I don’t expect its credit rating to be downgraded from investment-grade in the near term, I think higher financial leverage could lead credit agencies to review the company’s credit outlook ahead, as most of its cash flow generation is expected to be used for capital returns in 2026 instead of balance sheet deleveraging. While this is not an immediate threat to its dividend, it’s a reason, in my opinion, why Western Union’s dividend is not likely to be increased and may, eventually, be cut if the company does not reduce debt levels fast enough over the coming years.

The market seems to agree to some extent, given that Western Union is currently offering a dividend yield near 10%, which seems to be justified by fundamental issues rather than an opportunity for income investors.

A good example of a company that has for some time protected its dividend despite business woes and has now decided to cut the dividend following a credit outlook revision is LyondellBasell (LYB), which I covered a few months ago and saw its dividend as not being sustainable. LyondellBasell was also offering a very high dividend yield, around 10-11% recently, but it decided today to cut its dividend nearly in half, a move that seems to have largely been motivated by fears of a potential credit downgrade.

While I see Western Union’s financial profile as being stronger than LyondellBasell, the company is using too much free cash flow to return capital to shareholders while financial leverage is expected to increase at the same time. I think the company will need to cut the dividend to save cash over the next couple of years, especially considering that its high-dividend yield doesn’t seem to be acting as a floor for its share price.

On the other hand, Western Union’s guidance for 2026 is acceptable and close to market expectations, which is a good sign regarding its operating momentum over the coming quarters. The company has some growth initiatives expected to be launched over the coming months that could help to turn around its business, plus the acquisition of Intermex is expected to add some $650 million in annual revenues. Growth initiatives include the launch of its USD-backed stablecoin during the first half of this year, plus recent agreements with Canada Post and Deutsche Post to distribute Western Union’s services throughout their customer networks are potentially important factors for improving revenues during the second half of 2026.

Given this background of organic growth efforts and the integration of Intermex, its guidance is to reach adjusted revenue growth between 6-9% in 2026. While this seems much better than what the company has reported lately, investors should be aware that the vast majority of revenue growth comes from the integration of Intermex, and organic growth should be quite modest. Plus, the company is also expecting some stabilization in its core remittances business, which may be somewhat optimistic in my opinion.

Conclusion

Western Union continues to report a relatively weak operating momentum as the core business continues to be a drag on the company’s top-line performance, a trend that is not expected to change much ahead. While the company is taking some positive steps to diversify its business and be less reliant on the traditional channels, the structural shift of money transfers to digital is not expected to slow down ahead, being a major headwind for its growth prospects. Therefore, I continue to see its low valuation and high-dividend yields as a trap and maintain my ‘Hold’ recommendation for the time being.

data.title: Peyto: Keeping The Shareholders Informed
data.description:
Ever since Peyto (PEYUF) made the largest acquisition in some time a couple of years ago, they have kept shareholders informed of the progress of the acquisition. But even before that, they have provided shareholders with more easy-to-understand company statistics than many companies I follow. The latest report is the reserves report, which backs up that the acquisition lowered costs. Moreover, that land had a fair amount of liquids rich production. Another thing that management has repeatedly noted is some of the intervals produced on that acquired acreage also extend to the legacy acreage, where they have indeed been found to be profitable as well. The result is an ever-growing inventory of acreage with reserves to produce for the future. More importantly, the reserve costs are as good as or lower than in the past, which is helping the company beat inflation.

Hedging

Also covered in this report is the hedging activity reported by the company. Management reported the highest hedging levels in some time. Remember that this does not "fix" the sales prices, as management can (and sometimes does) send the natural gas to a stronger market than the hedging would indicate to pocket the difference after settling the hedges. It was not that long ago that management purchased gas at negative prices to bring to market and then settled the hedges to pocket the difference. AECO frequently goes negative for short periods of time. This management is flexible enough to deal with that when it happens.

Put the activity mentioned as a vote towards relatively good pricing that was worth "forward selling" the production to have some revenue predictability at good prices.

It is not necessarily a perfect forecast. Remember that this is a company that often lags a natural gas pricing recovery by about a year as a result of the hedging activity. But over time, the hedging here has produced that rare profit that makes it part of the money-making strategy. For this company, hedging is not a zero-sum game.

While the company often lags a cyclical recovery, at the same time it often breezes through cyclical downturns as a result.

But the key difference here is the overall long-term profit of the hedging strategy.

Production

Additionally, this company focuses on bringing on new production in time for the very strong pricing that exists during the heating season. This often adds a couple of percent to the ROI for the well drilled. There are more than a few companies out there that bring on a lot of production during season times of weak prices. This company regularly makes sure the odds favor a superior return.

The last article mentioned the company often drills more speculative field extension and discovery wells in the first half of the fiscal year. The data acquired from those activities often dictates a much more conservative strategy during the second half of the fiscal year that enables cost goals to be met (if they were not met already).

The company reports all wells drilled in the company presentation for shareholders to see. It is that rare opportunity for investors to understand just how much variance happens on "known" acreage.

In any event, January came about pretty much as planned with record production. However, from here on in, production will seasonally decline as shown in the past because management is busy with exploration and lease extension, as well as testing new intervals for viability.

There is also going to be an emphasis in the spring and summer on drilling. Completion begins really in the third quarter. The new wells come on as natural gas prices rise for the heating season. But that means generally by Christmas this company is uncharacteristically (for a Canadian company) done with the year's activity. After the holidays, the riskier activity begins, with standard drilling activity (for the fall completion) to follow.

Debt

The president's letter also delves into the debt strategy of this conservatively run company. The last acquisition took debt to record levels (for this company anyway). But management has justified that debt through measurements to shareholders ever since.

Management does note that the debt has to be serviced during cyclical downturns. It also noted that debt has to be repaid no matter what happens. Hence the hedging strategy discussed before to provide visibility to a minimal level of cash flow.

In this case the debt levels are reduced. But the debt ratio is also attacked by raising production profitability (with some level of guarantee due to the hedging).

Right now, this company is about average.

However, plans are underway through both increasing production and repaying some debt to decrease the interest expense further. The hedging program adds some surety to this forecast, as it is a bit less dependent upon commodity prices because of that hedging program. The fact that the hedging program is profitable in the long-term is "icing on the cake".

Summary

This management uncharacteristically leveraged up for the Repsol acreage acquisition. That resulted in increased interest expense per unit, as shown above. Now supposedly it was more than made up for by a roughly 20% cost advantage of the new acreage. That cost advantage has got intervals that extend to legacy acreage with the same cost advantage.

But this is the kind of acquisition that enables both a dividend combined with production growth and debt reduction. The hedging program is there to reduce the risk of such a strategy. Remember, there are many in the industry that repay debt as fast as possible in a situation like this and do not consider hedging as risk reduction (as is the case here).

This company remains a strong buy idea in that there are likely to be years of growth from that acquisition made a couple of years back. A management that bothers to justify a large acquisition like the Repsol (REPYY) acreage is a management that is not going to sweep issues (under the rug). This is a management that is likely to report back the good, the bad, and the ugly on a regular basis. Such management generally tackles whatever has happened to keep things going in the right direction. If anything, they increase the safety of the investment.

I would tend to hang on to this idea unless the story changes materially or the stock gets way overpriced. I continue to believe in the long run that the total return here is likely to be in the teens.

Risks

There is always the chance that leverage will not be an advantage in the future.

Similarly, there is the risk that technology advances could shift low-cost acreage to another location or basin.

The fact that the hedging program has shown a long-term profit does not mean there will not be years that show hedging losses. What it does mean is that the profits outweigh the losses in the long term.

It is possible that in the future, the hedging program will not be profitable. This management does have several ways to deal with the AECO volatility that often leads to decent profits compared to AECO. But there is always a chance that any or all of the strategies will fail in the future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: indie Semiconductor: Still Waiting On Inflection
data.description:
indie Semiconductor, Inc. (INDI) reported mixed Q4'25 results and gave the stock market a solid, reinforced view on the potential of the company. The auto tech continues to report a massive backlog and opportunities in humanoid robots and quantum computing, but the actual financial results aren't improving. My investment thesis remains ultra bullish on the stock, but indie Semiconductor has to get back into growth mode to make this stock work.

End Of A Disappointing 2025

indie Semiconductor reported the following Q4'25 results:

The company reported a quarterly beat of nearly $1 million, but sales were flat YoY. indie Semiconductor even announced some promising contracts to build on the current $7.4 billion backlog. Unfortunately, the massive backlog just isn't adding to actual revenue growth yet.

Even the revenue breakout from the sale of the WuXi indie Microelectronics unit is a mixed view. indie Semiconductor guided to Q1'26 revenues of only $55 million, with $34 million coming from the core business growing 20% sequentially.

The sales story is still supposed to get better over time. On the Q4'25 earnings call, CEO Donald McClymont highlighted the following Tier 1 radar contract ramp taking place in 2026 as follows:

We began initial shipments to our Tier 1 partner in December as planned and are scaling production to fulfill the massive opportunity estimated at well above 50 million units annual demand once we are beyond the ramp-up phase. To support this ramp and mitigate allocation issues, we're expanding our production capabilities, including porting designs to second source foundries here in the U.S., satisfying local supply sourcing demands. We are also securing additional back end and test capacity at multiple suppliers to be prepared for the ramp. With these measures in place, indie will be well positioned to fulfill the growing demand.

The radar deal started ramping up in December, so the 20% sequential growth amounts to around $6 million in additional revenue in Q1. The WuXi indie Microelectronics business is slipping from nearly $30 million in Q4 down to the guidance of $21 million in Q1.

The most important aspect is that indie sold the WuXi business for $135 million in cash while the unit was upwards of 50% of sales. The market has been waiting for the auto tech company to ramp up towards the $800 million revenue target for 2028 and this leaves indie Semiconductor with an even smaller revenue base.

The management team reinforced financial goals for the radar product to add $100 million in additional annual revenues. Back on the Q4'25 earnings call, CEO Donald McClymont had this answer to the revenue target question:

I mean, I think, the answer to your question is, yes, we are getting continued visibility improvement in this as we progress through the whole process of deployment. I mean we're -- it's probably a little early to call exactly when -- what date that we cross $100 million. But I mean, we are feeling increasingly confident and positive about where we're going with this right now.

The stock has a market cap of $775 million with a future cash balance to offset most of the $340 million in existing debt. Once the WuXi indie Microelectronics deal closes, indie Semiconductor will have a combined cash balance of $290 million, with the deal adding $135 million when closing towards year end.

indie Semiconductor only reported a $10 million loss during the quarter, so a big ramp-up in sales will help eliminate the losses. The radar business is forecast to add about $30 to $50 million in sales during 2026 on the path to a total value of at least $100 million, though sales of 50 million units should provide additional revenues.

Beyond the auto tech sector, quantum computing sales are forecast to grow from $1 million last year to $3 million in 2026 while the humanoid opportunity is still developing. indie Semiconductor doesn't lack growth catalysts.

The consensus analyst estimates only forecast 2026 revenues of $270 million with a rise to $367 million in 2027. indie Semiconductor still has the backlog to support prior big revenue targets, and the backlog doesn't include any material revenues for the new categories.

The stock is back to the recent lows. The risk is definitely that indie Semiconductor continues churning quarterly sales in the mid-$50 million range and the stock slumps after the WuXi revenues are removed later in the year. Unless the company truly is producing far higher revenue from the radar product this year, the market is unlikely to react favorably to a business watching reported revenues fall back to levels from a few years ago.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that indie Semiconductor has strong catalysts in the auto tech sector and new emerging markets like quantum computing and humanoid robots. The stock remains cheap here, but the company has to deliver the higher Q2/Q3 sales targets, or the market will lose interest.

data.title: National Energy Services Reunited Targets MENA And Asia's Rapid Industrialization And Urbanization
data.description:
Buy Rating Reflects Positive Sentiment Towards NESR

This analysis aligns with the current market sentiment on the NASDAQ for National Energy Services Reunited Corp. (NESR) stock, up 55% YTD. Wall Street is generally bullish on this stock, as MarketWatch indicates:

NESR stands out in the energy sector as a US-listed company with a strategic position in the Middle East, North Africa, and Asia Pacific regions. Based on this discussion's focus on the dynamic high-growth trends in these regions and the company's financial position, we ultimately expect to see evidence of the company's ability to capture share in these markets, supporting a distinctly bullish sentiment towards NESR shares.

About the Business of NESR

Founded in 2017, NESR is a leading provider of oilfield services in the MENA region (Middle East and North Africa) and the Asia-Pacific region. These services, made possible by a workforce of 7,000 employees in 16 countries, encompass reservoir potential optimization processes, including hydraulic fracturing, cementing, coiled tubing, filtration, completion, and stimulation.

The company also offers drilling and evaluation services such as directional drilling, drilling fluids, wireline services, and rig services, thus supporting its clients in the more efficient development of reservoirs.

NESR's Diverse Service Portfolio

Based on recent business results and trends, the combination of reservoir potential optimization services with well services—the core of NESR's production-oriented offering—plays a more central role in the company's profitability and future growth prospects. While drilling and evaluation services also contribute significantly to these two objectives, they currently play a subordinate role.

NESR's Portfolio by Region: Key Macroeconomic, Geopolitical, and Market Trends

These are the key macroeconomic, geopolitical, and market trends we have identified in each of the two geographic regions where NESR provides its services.

MENA Region

It has long been known that the MENA region (Middle East and North Africa) is among the world's largest oil producers. Anyone who hears or reads about countries in this region, such as Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Iraq, immediately thinks of oil and its ubiquitous production. The economies of these countries are heavily dependent on oil production, and to maintain this tradition, they rely on services and technologies like those continuously provided by NESR. NESR markets these services and contributes to preserving the MENA region's oil tradition and its strong, charismatic appeal, which persists despite the expansion of renewable energies and, together with them, continues to drive the global economy and growth. One market does not exclude another; this is the principle of an economy whose ultimate goal is to grow through the interconnectedness of markets in a world that, in the face of geopolitical tensions, is finding ever greater reasons for globalization. Amidst this diversity, the opportunity for dialogue arises. Despite inevitable conflicts and friction, this offers an opportunity to improve and strengthen relationships in the long term. Paradoxically, even protectionist tariff policies aim for this goal: they help economies adapt to strategies that best meet constant change—and thereby become more resilient and future-proof, for the benefit of society and the economy. This strengthens domestic and foreign companies, which are increasingly forming stable alliances.

The MENA Region: The Crucial Role of NESR Comes Into Play

Investors who closely monitor all markets are aware of the opportunities arising from the heterogeneity and diversification of resources—for example, the diverse energy generation from renewable sources as well as from oil, natural gas, and fossil fuels. To power an energy-intensive world with its AI data centers, electric vehicles and batteries, electrification, and defense technologies, the MENA regions, with their rich heritage of oil and gas as primary resources, play a crucial role. To secure their strong position in oil production, MENA governments are investing in efficiency improvement programs, including fracking, well stimulation, and advanced reservoir technologies. This is precisely where NESR's production services come into play. This is how the situation appears from an investor's perspective and aligns with the demands of a global economy that must grow while simultaneously becoming more efficient—with maximum results and minimal resource consumption.

Mammoth Agreements in the MENA region

The following are some of the key initiatives to secure the country's oil and gas supply in the region, which raise high expectations for robust demand for the services promoted by NESR:

The United Arab Emirates' unconventional oil and gas reserves, for which a drilling contract worth US$1.7 billion was signed in May 2024, are being developed by three partners—one in the Middle East and two in the USA; similar projects could follow.

Eni and BP, long-standing leading operators in the Egyptian energy sector, have been commissioned by EGPC and EGAS to advance natural gas discoveries in the Mediterranean, including exploration of the Temsah concession area. This opens up growth opportunities for operators like NESR, strategically positioned in the region with a wide range of production and well services.

The Asia-Pacific Region

The second region on which NESR focuses is experiencing rapid industrialization and strong population growth in countries such as India, China, and Southeast Asia, accompanied by rising energy demand. This is particularly true for China, which aims to overtake the US in the global GDP rankings and become the world's leading economy. However, neither China nor the other energy-hungry countries of the Asia-Pacific region can rely too heavily on foreign energy resources, as this would strain their budgets and jeopardize the achievement of their goals. Instead, they must develop their own extensive oil and gas reserves. Global markets, which consider the economic aspects of geopolitics, are favorably disposed toward the scenario of these countries developing their own oil fields, provided this is done with efficient well services and appropriate evaluation methods to optimize production. In exchange for less reliance on imported products, the technologies of Western companies like NESR, based in Houston, Texas, and with a strategic presence in the Asia-Pacific region, would have gained more ground in an environment of exploitation opportunities that has hopefully become fairer for all oil company service providers, both domestic and foreign, as a result of the global trade war initiated by US President Donald Trump.

The Enormous Potential of the Asia-Pacific region

Examples of demand drivers for NESR services: A) The development of reserves in challenging, dense formations for new shale oil and large-scale gas production in the Sichuan Basin, which is of strategic importance for China's energy security. B) Maintaining production increases in mature fields, such as providing additional oil and gas volumes in the offshore areas such as Mumbai and the leading role of the Assam region in the Indian oil industry. C) The drilling operations must penetrate complex geological formations such as those in the Beetaloo Basin, which makes the project technically very challenging due to its unconventional gas resources, but Australia is rich in such rock types.

These are just a few examples of the opportunities that the Asia-Pacific region offers for specialized production services such as NESR's well stimulation and reservoir optimization services.

National Energy Services: Q4 2025 Results, The Situation

Seeking Alpha reports on ACCESS Newswire on February 17, 2026, and reveals NESR's key financial results for Q4 2025 Results:

Revenue was driven by improved capacity utilization and the awarding of new contracts. Adjusted EBITDA, adjusted earnings, and operating cash flow all contributed consistently to the growth trajectory. The adjusted EBITDA margin of 21.2% remained essentially unchanged compared to the previous quarter, meaning that NESR can sustain this expansion without increasing operating costs. Sequential trends show positive momentum, building on year-over-year progress, which is good.

Net profit has not improved compared to previous periods, as it includes non-cash impairment charges on minor technology investments and provisions related to the insolvency of other companies.

Rosy Outlook for NESR

In a comment on NESR's results for the fourth quarter of 2025, Stefan Angeli, Chief Financial Officer, said:

[…] As we enter 2026, our focus remains on executing our growing backlog efficiently, maintaining strong margins, improving working capital performance, and further enhancing returns on capital. We believe NESR is uniquely positioned to enter its next phase of growth with record revenues, strong operational momentum, and a clear focus on delivering durable, long-term value to shareholders.

Shareholder Value, Large-Scale Growth In Various Countries

Sherif Foda, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, added:

We are very pleased with our fourth quarter results, which cap another year of disciplined execution and strategic progress for NESR. The quarter follows the announcement of several significant contract awards, most notably our integrated unconventional completions scope in Saudi Arabia's Jafurah development, which further reinforces our long-standing partnership with Aramco and our role in enabling world-class unconventional operations in the Kingdom. […]. Importantly, we continue to see strong momentum beyond Saudi Arabia, with new awards across the broader MENA region supporting a multi-year growth trajectory. As we enter 2026, NESR is shifting to a totally different gear and scale, operating from a position of strength, with multiple opportunities spanning across different countries, and a highly committed team. We remain focused on delivering consistent value to our customers, shareholders, and partners, and I am extremely proud of what our people have accomplished.

Non-GAAP free cash flow for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2025, was $120.8 million, compared to $124.2 million in the prior year. This decrease is temporary, as the company is increasing its capital expenditures, which we estimate to be $143.4 million in 2025, compared to $105.1 million in 2024. NESR has identified the right time for growth and is taking advantage of it. If not now, when is the right time, then, to increase spending on growth investments? Certainly not while the business is stagnant. Current opportunities, particularly in the MENA region and the Asia-Pacific region, are promising, and NESR is rightly investing in new equipment and technology to support its service segments growth. Specifically, services to optimize reservoir potential (including hydraulic fracturing, cementing, coiled tubing, filtration, completion, and stimulation) and well services aimed at helping customers develop reservoirs more efficiently. Judging by the share price uptrend, shareholders are confident that these actions will deliver the desired results, including improved annual free cash flow and other profitability metrics that are currently lagging behind annual revenue growth. But if revenue growth is expected to translate into higher profits, and the year-on-year unchanged adjusted EBITDA margins indicate that NESR has costs under control, it is only a matter of time until the next step is that the desired improvements will be visible in other areas, where profitability will also make important strides.

Financial Situation

The previous paragraph suggests that the financial condition is ready to absorb the beneficial inflows. Will this current state of the financial situation—that is, the snapshot as of September 30, 2025 – already be sufficient to support NESR during this phase of very rapid, hyperbolic growth? It is now crucial to maintain a sound, efficient, and competitive financial position, to cover expenses, and to secure all necessary opportunities, because, as CFO Stefan Angeli explains:

During 2025, we continued to prioritize cash generation and debt reduction, ending the year with Net Debt of $185.3 million, down nearly $90 million year-over-year, and a significantly strengthened liquidity position. As we enter 2026, our focus remains on executing our growing backlog efficiently, maintaining strong margins, improving working capital performance, and further enhancing returns on capital.

Total debt (short-term and long-term debt) amounted to US$310.1 million as of December 31, 2025, compared to US$382.8 million as of December 31, 2024.

About the balance sheet, the company stated:

The decrease in Net Debt reflects scheduled long-term debt repayments made during 2025, more than offset by a larger magnitude of free cash flow generation.

Considerations About the Financial Situation

NESR is able to repay its debt on time, which strengthens the market's confidence in the company's financial strength and also positively affects its credit rating, should the company need to raise additional debt to support growth or exploit new opportunities. In relation to these opportunities, look at what is happening in the MENA and Asia-Pacific regions - a very dynamic environment. It cannot be said that the risk is fully assessed in relation to the magnitude of these opportunities that may arise in such a dynamic picture of the MENA region and the Asia-Pacific region, and that NESR may at any given time wish to capture. This picture allows us to imagine a stable but immeasurable growth, a dense growth but fraught with fierce competition. In order to remain and operate dynamically for long enough and seize as many opportunities as possible, it may be necessary to go a little further with risk and adopt a more courageous, entrepreneurial, and adventurous attitude.

Given the outlook for NESR's current financial situation, we believe this provides shareholders with a solid basis to be reasonably optimistic about the repercussions if NESR moves towards a more adventurous way of doing business.

Financial Leverage Ratios

The following information is based on NESR's previous quarterly reports:

Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $84.4 million.

Q3 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $64.0 million.

Q2 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $70.6 million.

Q1 2025 adjusted EBITDA was $62.5 million.

Adjusted EBITDA for the last twelve months (LTM) was $281.5 million.

With debt of $310.1 million, the debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio for the last twelve months was 1.1. This ratio indicates that the company is not over-indebted and that earnings can cover liabilities—a positive signal for investors and lenders. NSER is in a significantly better position compared to many competitors, as GuruFocus indicates a median value of 1.925 for the ratio of net debt to EBITDA for the industry.

NESR's interest coverage ratio (LTM) is 3 (= Seeking Alpha’s LTM NESR’s Operating Income/Seeking Alpha’s LTM Interest Expense), which, while not as high as the industry median of 5.67 reported by GuruFocus, is still well above the minimum tolerable level of 1.5 to 2.0. NESR is therefore able to cover the interest payments due on its outstanding debt.

Although the Altman Z Score of 1.56 (see the “Risk” section on Seeking Alpha) indicates uncertainties in the grey areas, the index, as this analysis shows, is on a good path of improvement and should therefore not be a cause for concern for investors.

NESR National Energy Services Reunited Corp. On The NASDAQ

Shares of National Energy Services Reunited Corp., which trade on the NASDAQ under the symbol “NESR,” were quoted at $4.58 per share, representing a market capitalization of $486.89 million.

Technical Analysis

The stock price has fluctuated between $5.20 and $25.21 per share over the past 52 weeks. The average of this range is $15.205 per share. Currently, NESR stock is trading 59.2% above this 52-week average. The 20-day moving average is above the 50-day moving average, which in turn is above the 100-day moving average, and this is finally above the 200-day moving average. This orderly arrangement of trends on the chart suggests that the market is aiming for positive developments in the short, medium, and long term. The RSI is consistently at 74.16, which is close to overbought territory. The stock price is in this position because market sentiment is continuously pushing it toward this level. Therefore, NESR is not an overvalued stock. Although volatility can cause temporary price declines, the stock, as this analysis shows, offers growth potential.

Valuation

Compared to its direct competitors SLB, Halliburton Company, Weatherford International plc, and Liberty Energy Inc., NESR stock appears cheap in terms of its price-to-cash-flow ratio (TTM).

But measured by the EV/EBITDA (TTM) ratio, it is no longer the cheapest company in the group.

The conclusion regarding the NESR stock assessment is closely related to the first point of the following paragraph on risks, to which we now refer.

The Risks

These are the risks.

Hastily Drawn Conclusions Based On A Detached Assessment Of Financial Indicators

Since the EV/EBITDA ratio is higher than that of comparable companies, but the price-to-cash flow (P/CF) ratio is the lowest, one might hastily conclude that the stock is highly leveraged and that investors are undervaluing equity due to the risk associated with debt. This would explain the low P/CF. The company's total valuation (equity + debt) is high relative to the actual cash flow from its assets, which is reflected in the high EV/EBITDA ratio. This situation suggests that a company in the volatile oil and gas sector—as is often the case—may be exposed to greater risk than its peers during a market downturn. Therefore, the buy signal enters questionable zones.

Aside from the fact that the analysis of NESR's financial situation and the review of its debt ratios have shown that its debt is by no means risky, but rather indicates a stable and manageable debt profile, it strengthens NESR's long-term competitiveness as a service provider in multi-year contracts. The following chart from Seeking Alpha shows that NESR has the lowest debt level. Accordingly, the assumption that the visibly higher enterprise value (EV) compared to competitors, relative to EBITDA, is due to high debt, is incorrect.

The high enterprise value is instead attributable to the increase in NESR's share price on the Nasdaq, which has risen by 55% since the beginning of the year and by 157% in the last twelve months—much faster than comparable companies. Despite this, NESR's stock continues to have the lowest price-to-cash-flow ratio among its peers.

We are convinced that the market has a positive outlook for NESR's future prospects and that the company continues to have growth potential. A low price-to-cash-flow ratio suggests that the stock is still trading at attractive prices on the Nasdaq.

Oil and Gas Price Fluctuations

NESR's share price correlates with the oil and gas industry and its sensitivity to commodity price fluctuations. While the chart suggests this correlation is stronger for crude oil futures (CL1:COM) than for natural gas futures (NG1:COM), it cannot be said that NESR's share price fluctuations on the NASDAQ generally keep pace with commodity price fluctuations. As the chart created on TradingView shows, there are indeed long periods when the two securities move independently.

Rather, it is possible that NESR's share price is less volatile than the fluctuations in futures contracts, leading to less uncertainty in forecasts and some flexibility to adjust positions if new market developments necessitate a revised forecast. But when correlation is positive, a sharp drop in oil prices could prompt oil exploration and production companies to review their investment plans and potentially reduce an investment strategy that, in retrospect, appears disproportionate to the changed market conditions. Reduced spending on exploration and production activities could negatively impact demand for NESR's services, as projects might be postponed or canceled. Before the market realizes the effects of declining revenues and shrinking margins, these could already be priced into NESR's share price.

Instability In Key Markets

Instability in key markets, particularly in countries like Iraq and Libya, could lead to delays or cancellations of contracts, resulting in an undesirable loss of business opportunities. This is particularly true in Libya, which has developed into an area characterized by crime of all kinds.

Competition from larger, established companies does not currently pose a significant risk, as their leverage is no better than NESR's, which will be an advantage for NESR as the MENA and Asia-Pacific regions seek investments to help them develop their resources. But the oil and gas services industry remains highly competitive, and companies like Schlumberger, Halliburton, and Baker Hughes could hinder NESR's efforts to maintain and expand its market share. This would be unfortunate given the very positive forecasts provided by current analyses of future scenarios for the markets in the Middle East, North Africa, and the Asia-Pacific region.

Conclusion

NESR offers promising growth prospects, particularly due to the strong outlook stemming from the potential operational momentum in the MENA region and the Asia-Pacific area. Furthermore, the company can leverage its solid financial position, which, combined with higher utilization rates and the signing of new contracts, creates even more favorable conditions for further revenue growth. Effective debt management with a low net debt ratio (net debt/EBITDA) ensures financial stability. There are also prospects for improved cash flow and higher profitability, as well as disciplined cost management and the expansion of market share in key sectors. Thanks to ongoing expansion opportunities and the positive outlook for the energy services market, NESR is ideally positioned for continued success, making it a compelling buy for investors seeking exposure to the sector.

data.title: Ames National: Spreads Are Widening Again
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Regional banks have benefited from a widening in interest rate spreads after two years of pressure. Ames National (ATLO), a small, Iowa-based community banking holding company, saw its shares up 21% YTD. This is a particularly impressive performance for a slow-compounder with low beta.

This buy rating is based on ATLO's revenue and profitability growth combined with the stock price momentum, as the spread between what ATLO pays on deposits and what it earns on loans widens.

Recent Performance

The growth in ATLO's net income is impressive. Net income rose by 85% in Q4'25, exceeding $6.5 million. ROA almost doubled, reaching 1.25% in Q4'25, up from 0.66% in the same period of the prior year.

Net interest margins improved, rising from 2.38% to 3% as of December 2025. This helped interest income rise by 3.5% to $17.6 million despite the loan portfolio declining to $1.29 billion, down from $1.31 billion in Q4'24.

In other words, ATLO earns more on a smaller portfolio with higher yields.

In recent years, we've seen ATLO's net income become more volatile, as monetary policy changes have become more abrupt. Prior to the pandemic, back to the Great Depression, net income hovered mostly in the $3 to $4 million region. After the pandemic, ATLO reported quarterly net income between $2.1 and $6.7 million.

At the same time, interest expense on deposits dropped to $6.9 million, down from $7.9 million, despite deposits increasing. The decline in deposit expense accelerated in Q4'25, reflecting easing competition for deposits and the impact of the Fed's rate cuts in recent quarters.

Balance Sheet

ATLO's loan portfolio declined slightly in Q4'25 to an average balance of $1.29 billion, down from $1.31 billion in the same period of the prior year. This was partially offset by a decline in Investment Securities to $656.2 million, down from $668.5 million. Overall, total interest-earning assets were down/flattish at $2.04 billion, compared to $2.05 billion in the same period of 2024.

What is interesting is that yields across the loan book rose in Q4'25 across all asset classes except high-yield agricultural loans. Rates on real estate loans, the largest asset class, went up by 33 basis points, reaching an average rate of 5.18% in the quarter, up from 4.85% in Q4'24. Despite the Fed rate hikes, rates on long-term loans haven't fully tracked short-term rates. In other words, the yield curve has steepened. The decrease in deposit costs in Q4'25 reflects these dynamics. The average yield on interest-bearing deposits was down 30 basis points from 2.15% to 1.84% in Q4'25.

The company's results are particularly encouraging given ATLO's exposure to the agriculture sector. Iowa ranks #1 in corn production in the US. In 2025, corn prices ended lower despite an encouraging first half of the year.

In terms of exposure, ATLO has significant exposure to the agricultural sector. Roughly 22% of the company's loan assets are for the agriculture sector.

Challenges in the agricultural sector naturally impacted ATLO's portfolio. Classified assets, which track loans that management deems as having elevated risk, increased to 2.7% of total assets in Q4'25, up from 2.3% just in the prior quarter.

Dividends

The growth in earnings and the widening of spreads came after a period of margin and rate spread contraction. In 2024, ATLO cut its dividend to maintain financial flexibility. Today, a dividend increase is possible. However, management can also decide to maintain the current distribution level, exercising patience before rushing into raising dividends. Historically, ATLO adopted a stable-dividend strategy.

In Q4'25, the board authorized the purchase of 200,000 common shares through November 2026. Management exercised 34,947 of this figure, leaving 165,053 shares left under the buyback program.

Valuation & Outlook

This year, ATLO forecasts EPS between $2.7 and $2.8 per share. That's a significant growth compared to $2.14 reported in 2025. Management cited a continuation of the rate spread tailwinds as funding costs decline while lending income increases, or at least stays flat. Based on 2026 EPS estimates, ATLO trades at a forward P/E ratio of 10.1x, with the share price now trading at $27.9/share.

In terms of valuation, ATLO seems to fall in line with peers' average.

Risks

The primary catalyst for this buy thesis is the widening rate spreads. Management's 2025 net income estimates seem to reflect confidence that the current monetary environment remains accommodative to profits. However, the reality is that spreads can narrow quickly according to monetary conditions.

Although ATLO runs 6 banking institutions, all of its business is concentrated in Iowa. Below are the banks operating under the ATLO umbrella:

  • Boone Bank & Trust
  • First National Bank
  • Iowa State Savings Bank
  • Reliance State Bank
  • State Bank & Trust
  • United Bank & Trust

This regional concentration creates risk. In recent quarters, Iowa, which relies heavily on agricultural exports, was caught up in international trade wars and tariffs, making it harder for farmers to trade their produce. In 2025, agricultural commodity prices also fell. Although ATLO's assets are predominantly real estate, it does have exposure to the agricultural sector. Today's Supreme Court ruling against Trump's global tariffs is constructive to the geopolitical backdrop. However, international trade agreements will continue to impact Iowa's farmers.

Final Thoughts

The difference between when ATLO's banks pay for deposits and what they earn on loans has expanded. This is because deposit rates are set based on short-term rates, while loans are naturally longer-term.

The spread change is a reflection of broader monetary dynamics. The shape of the yield curve has become steeper. A steepening yield curve is often a sign that the market expects short-term interest to fall, while inflation expectations for the future period grow. A bet on ATLO is a bet that these dynamics will continue.

data.title: Vishay Precision: AI Opportunity Keeping The Story Alive, But I'm Remaining Skeptical
data.description:
Vishay Precision Group (VPG) is an industrial technology designer and manufacturer, with its 3 key revenue generators being sensors, weighing solutions, and measurement systems. Sentiment has been negative for VPG post-earnings, with the RSI immediately pulling back from oversold territory as investors seem to dislike the most recent quarter, just after VPG hit an all-time high at the start of the month. I believe that VPG's valuation is now slightly inflated after the recent run up, as the valuation is now slightly less attractive for an entry point despite a ~20% pullback already, but opportunities lie ahead for VPG. Hence, I'm rating VPG at a 'Hold.'

Thesis

There are multiple catalysts for VPG for 2026, with a big part of the next phase of AI being humanoids clearly benefiting VPG with their orders as well as potential margin expansion, which could lead to VPG generating more customers. This tends to lead to a long-term relationship since VPG is a B2B focusing on specialised products, as the stickiness and potential partnerships enforce VPG's moat. An emphasis on specialist sensors on humanoids for 2026 is a huge catalyst going forward, as the TAM for robotics is massive, especially compared to VPG's current size.

VPG has rallied over the last year, with its stock price gaining over 75% despite a recent ~20% pullback directly post earnings. The rally was led with optimism for VPG's future with solid new orders from business development initiatives as well as being undervalued 12 months ago. Valuations are now rather mixed, which doesn't give a good entry point for investors, as the value play last year is no longer really a value play, as valuations are already higher than historical averages. Unless VPG soars to high valuations like the tech sector, which may be possible if their humanoid book-to-bill gains massive momentum. Though VPG isn't really an AI company, which means that it doesn't have AI valuations, a change in sentiment or acceleration in growth regarding AI could lead to higher valuations, but the size of the business is nowhere near enough to justify this, as their target for 2026 for new orders from business development initiatives, which is boosted by humanoid robotics-related orders, is still a small size of their revenue.

Q4 2025 Review And Looking Forward

Q4 reported $80.6M in revenue and adjusted EPS of $0.07 per share, beating analyst top line expectations but missing bottom line expectations of $0.21 per share, a huge 66% miss. Adjusted EBITDA also missed analyst expectations of $7.85M by 23.6%, reporting just $6M in Q4, which is also another huge miss. VPG has now maintained 5 consecutive quarters of at least a 1 book-to-bill, which suggests for now that demand is stable, since they are shipping the same amount to new orders every quarter. This is not necessarily a bad thing since this is signalling that VPG customers are demanding more products for projects, and the potential for acceleration in their bookings is mostly carried by sensors, as a low book-to-bill only gives VPG room to grow as it is already a relatively low number as it gets more exposed to the AI sector.

Sensor bookings are now regaining momentum, though revenue fell sequentially, which is not a big deal as their book-to-bill, which is, in my opinion, a more important trend for future growth, continues to gain momentum and now stands at 1.15, which is important for VPG as sensors is their largest segment of the 3 and stood at 37% of revenue in Q4.

VPG also focuses on different aspects of the humanoid itself, as they mention in their conference with Jefferies:

On customer number one, though, we are designed in, we believe, in terms of our sensors in the joints of the robot. So it's the shoulders, the elbows, the wrist, etcetera. And it and these, sensors are playing a critical role in the functioning of this robot.

The second customer we're now engaged with providing sensors in the hands of the robot. So essentially providing the tactile feel of the robot, enabling that robot to actually perform delicate tasks in one moment and then something, something else in another moment.

Though it isn't really clear just yet which part VPG will have a focus on, VPG is focused on advanced strain gauge sensors, which it already has experience in in many different key industries such as aerospace and defence, automotive design, manufacturing, and engineering. Humanoids are still at a rather early phase, so VPG is still positioned quite well if it continues to build momentum for new orders, as we are still in the stage of R&D where firms are trying to develop humanoids that can actually be brought to market for different use cases. The main question now is whether VPG can continue to gain momentum in 2026, as new customers and orders from new developers are a key thing to look out for.

I believe that this is VPG's most exciting segment, as though VPG doesn't really have exposure to the AI segment, their specialised sensors could accelerate with design wins and new orders from humanoid projects, as humanoids would play a large part in the next phase of the AI revolution. Projections from Morgan Stanley analysts had the TAM for humanoid processors and sensors at $305B by 2045, though obviously still 2 decades away, the size of the TAM should simply show the potential for TAM to capitalise on just sensors. With humanoid orders only being $4M in 2025 and now expanding to their third humanoid developer customer, this could be the start for VPG to establish relationships as it emphasises lifetime relationships for their humanoid sensors, as they mention on their website when describing the StrainBond (3 links above). New customers benefit VPG as their back-and-forth improvements in sensors would lead to better knowledge of customer requirements, and if the competition starts to heat up, increased spending/R&D on humanoid testing would also benefit VPG, increasing their backlog.

I expect continued acceleration in revenue and a larger book-to-bill in Q1 2026, as a higher book-to-bill in Q4 than Q3 likely already suggests growth in revenue from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026, so the main emphasis falls back on book-to-bill in Q1 2026, as the trend and momentum have to be clearer as VPG's metrics across the board have been rather rocky. Humanoid orders are also expected to grow in 2026, with $1M already at the start of the year, as a clearer number in the next report for Q1 will make it easier to estimate new orders for 2026, as January 2026 alone has already achieved 25% of the orders of 2025 alone. I believe the next few reports are very important for VPG as well as being very volatile, as investors try to figure out whether VPG deserves AI valuations and their exposure to AI. Even though the answer is basically no due to their extremely limited exposure, it is still at early stages, and getting in now could be extremely smart if traction accelerates and VPG gains momentum in orders.

Weighing solutions in Q4 stood at 34% of revenue, as it is a relatively stable segment and is also affected by cyclicality. Revenue has declined compared to 2 years ago, with Q4 2023 revenue at $30.4M and Q4 2024 revenue at $25.7M, which has now recovered to $27.7M. There isn't really a catalyst here for VPG, as recovery from cyclicality has been rather weak. As though it is stable, it is very choppy, which means there isn't a real trend looking forward. With book-to-bill now trending upwards, VPG needs to continue this trend to justify its valuation, as a third of the business being choppy is not exactly bullish when only 1/3 segments are only having catalysts while the other 2 continue to remain heavily cyclical.

Measurement systems bookings are currently on a decline at a low book-to-bill similar to the prior year, so though the huge drop seems worrying, it is still higher than the book-to-bill 1 year prior. I believe that the next quarter is very important to determine if what they say actually comes to light, that the timing of projects leads to lower bookings, and if a similar trend will happen like the prior year, where Q4 is slightly weak and it recovers from Q1-Q3. If bookings start to decline in Q1, it will likely suggest that the revenue trend may decline in the short term. If sensors don't continue to outperform expectations and book-to-bill trending upwards, measurement systems weakness will be a huge problem looking forward.
